source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Democracy depends on voters rejecting antidemocratic candidates
Prior studies show voters prioritize party and policy over democracy in single-choice tradeoffs
We test whether expanding the choice environment to multiple races affects this tradeoff
Multirace ballots substantially increase punishment of antidemocratic candidates
The effect is robust across platforms, waves, and norm violations
Observational data from 2022 midterms corroborates experimental findings
Results suggest multirace elections may temper willingness to trade democracy for party and policy

typical_pattern: Open with broad question or problem statement
Elaborate with supporting evidence and robustness checks
Discuss implications for research and practice

likely_priorities: External validity and generalizability to real-world elections
Consistency between experimental and observational evidence
likely_attacks: Limited generalizability to executive races as focal race
Unclear whether effects hold when all party candidates adopt antidemocratic positions
Lack of evidence for proposed motivational mechanisms
Observational data challenges for causal inference
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges multiple limitations: 'whether our results extend to scenarios where the executive is the focal race we cannot say', 'Nor can our experiment answer whether the effects we uncover would apply to situations where all candidates within a party adopt explicitly antidemocratic positions', 'Although the nature of these data make causal inference challenging'. The authors also note that preregistered auxiliary hypotheses about checks and balances beliefs and moral licensing were not supported. They repeatedly call for 'future research' and 'Additional research should also be done on the specific motivational mechanisms'.

examples.quoted_phrase: Our study suggests that the answer is yes
the headline finding of our research is that when voters cast ballots with multiple races they are substantially more likely to punish individual antidemocratic candidates
our study suggests that it is the overall electoral choice context that alters preferences in the focal race
whether our results extend to scenarios where the executive is the focal race we cannot say
For citizens concerned with democratic backsliding, our results offer some reassurance

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
